text: The surgical setup includes a cadier forceps in the surgeon's right hand for retraction, a vessel sealer as the active instrument on the right side, and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand.
The Nathanson retractor is positioned to elevate and hold the left lobe of the liver, providing clear access to the surgical field.

The surgeon separates the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully maintaining proximity to the stomach to preserve the gastroepiploic vessels while ensuring precise hemostasis.
The surgeon navigates around vessels near the stomach, greater omentum, and short gastric arteries, taking care to avoid bleeding during dissection.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in the right hand and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand to perform the dissection, utilizing two energy sources for effective tissue separation.

The surgeon carefully dissects from the body towards the gastroesophageal junction, separating the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach. The short gastric vessels are encountered and efficiently divided using the vessel sealer.

The surgeon navigates the limited space below the enlarged left lobe of the liver and near the gastroesophageal junction in a patient with extreme BMI, using advanced instrumentation to perform precise surgical maneuvers.

The surgeon pulls down the last portion of the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring that the entire greater curvature is freed from its attachments, including the fundus.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully dissect the retrogastric tissues, which appear thicker than previously observed. The adhesions are flimsy, allowing for precise separation with the instrument.

The surgeon ensures that the fundus and gastroesophageal junction are completely freed from attachments at the splenic pole before proceeding with stapling to remove the redundant portion of the stomach.

The surgeon examines the gastroesophageal junction before shifting focus to the lower stomach, moving towards the pylorus. The separation is performed approximately five centimeters away from the pylorus in preparation for stapling.

The surgeon flexes the vessel sealer, bending it similarly to a hand or elbow, to manipulate and seal tissues with precision, demonstrating the enhanced maneuverability provided by robotic assistance.

The surgeon performs precise hemostatic maneuvers to remove the omentum from the surgical field, away from the greater curvature of the stomach. The remaining tissue is located approximately five centimeters from the pyloric region, preparing for further cutting.

The surgeon introduces a SureForm robotic stapler, demonstrating its articulating capability to create a sleeve of the stomach. A bougie is visible in place to prevent excessive narrowing during the stapling process. Indicators on the screen show the stapling is in progress, marking the first fire.

The surgeon uses both right-hand instruments to flatten the stomach, ensuring precise apposition of the anterior and posterior walls without rotation. The second stapler fire is executed, continuing the stapling process.

The surgeon flexes the robotic staplers towards the left side of the screen, following the natural curvature of the stomach to create a sleeve. The stapling device is progressively adjusted to ensure a precise staple line, with an average of seven to nine staplers used depending on the anatomy.

The surgeon continues stapling along the stomach to create a precise sleeve, ensuring division reaches the gastroesophageal junction. The fundus is carefully divided to prevent redundancy, using the belt of fat at the gastroesophageal junction as a visual marker for staple line placement.

The surgeon maneuvers the robotic camera to ensure precise visualization of the stomach while a bougie is in place to prevent narrowing of the sleeve. The surgeon performs flipping maneuvers to avoid pinching any tissue other than the stomach, conducting safety checks before each stapler firing.

The robotic stapler fires along the leftward tilt, completing the sleeve gastrectomy. The surgeon identifies the bell's east part of fat near the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring proximity to the junction. The final step involves the installation of diluted methylene blue to check for leaks along the staple line.

The surgeon performs a leak test by instilling 100 to 120 mL of fluid into the newly created sleeve gastrectomy to ensure the anastomosis is waterproof and leak-proof, confirming the sleeve's size and integrity.
